quick_facts.label: Credential and regulatory basis
quick_facts.value: M.Arch is the Council of Architecture (COA)-regulated postgraduate degree programme in architecture; COA’s 2022 postgraduate guidelines apply to M.Arch programmes from academic session 2023–24.

quick_facts.label: Standard duration
quick_facts.value: The full-time M.Arch programme has a minimum duration of 2 academic years / 4 semesters, with 18 working weeks (90 working days) in each semester. It ordinarily must be completed within 3 years, with a possible one-time additional year in special circumstances at the university or institution’s discretion.

quick_facts.label: Baseline eligibility
quick_facts.value: For full-time postgraduate admission, the COA guideline requires a B.Arch degree or equivalent with at least 50% aggregate marks or equivalent CGPA; applicable reservation and qualifying-mark relaxations follow the relevant Central or State policy.

quick_facts.label: Entrance requirement
quick_facts.value: Candidates must qualify an entrance test for postgraduate architecture. For the 2026–27 cycle, COA’s PGETA brochure states the same B.Arch/equivalent-and-50% baseline and directs candidates to qualify the prescribed PG entrance test; institutions and counselling authorities determine their own merit-weighting and admission processes.

quick_facts.label: Curriculum structure
quick_facts.value: COA prescribes a Choice Based Credit System framework with professional core courses, professional/open electives and employability or skill-enhancement courses. The suggested total is 75–85 credits across four semesters, including studio, thesis/tutorial/dissertation, theory, electives and optional workshop/lab work.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: The national baseline for a full-time M.Arch is a B.Arch degree, or an equivalent qualification, with at least 50% aggregate marks or equivalent CGPA. This is a minimum standard: a university, institute or counselling authority may publish additional programme-specific conditions, and reservation/relaxation rules are governed by the applicable Central or State policy.

Applicants should verify that both the institution and the specific M.Arch programme are approved or otherwise recognized as applicable. COA defines recognized architectural qualifications by reference to qualifications included in the Architects Act schedule or notified under the Act, and its postgraduate guidelines are intended for institutions imparting recognized qualifications.
bullets: Core entry qualification: B.Arch or equivalent.
Minimum academic threshold: 50% aggregate marks or equivalent CGPA.
Check the current institutional prospectus for specialization-specific conditions, document requirements and category-wise rules.

sections.heading: Duration and academic format
paragraphs: The COA’s current postgraduate guideline sets the full-time M.Arch minimum at two academic years over four semesters. Each semester is specified as 18 working weeks (90 working days). The programme is to be completed within a maximum of three years; in special circumstances, a university or institution may allow one additional year once, treated as a zero year.

COA’s 2022 guideline is specifically framed for full-time postgraduate degree programmes in architecture. Students should therefore treat claims about alternative formats or durations cautiously and check the approval status and current prospectus of the intended institution.
bullets: Semester length: 18 working weeks (90 working days).
Normal maximum completion window: 3 years, subject to the guideline’s exceptional extension provision.

sections.heading: Curriculum and specializations
paragraphs: M.Arch is a specialization-led programme rather than a single identical syllabus nationwide. COA’s framework uses a Choice Based Credit System: professional core courses are compulsory; professional and open electives provide specialization and cross-disciplinary breadth; and employability/skill-enhancement courses may be included. The suggested four-semester structure totals 75–85 credits and combines studio, thesis/tutorial/dissertation work, theory, electives, and optional workshops or labs.

COA identifies broad specialization clusters including architectural design; architectural science; energy/environment science; urbanism and cities; conservation; architectural history, theory and criticism; digital technology in buildings; construction management; and other focal areas. Examples within these clusters include landscape and infrastructure, building science, sustainable habitat and net-zero buildings, urban design and housing, heritage conservation, BIM/parametric design, and real-estate studies. Names, emphasis and individual subjects may vary by institution and regional context.

In addition to formal coursework, institutions are expected to incorporate activities suited to the programme, such as study tours, site visits and outstation studios, and to organize seminars, workshops, conferences and exhibitions. Assessment includes end-semester examinations and jury/panel assessment of sessional work, projects, design and research.
bullets: Suggested credit range: 75–85 credits over four semesters.
Typical components: advanced studio/design, theory, research or thesis/dissertation, electives and, where relevant, workshops/labs.
Compare programme curricula—not just degree titles—before applying, because COA permits institutions to formulate or modify courses to meet their objectives.

sections.heading: Admission process
paragraphs: COA requires candidates to qualify an entrance test for postgraduate architecture. Its 2026 PGETA brochure presents PGETA as the entrance route for first-year admission to the two-year postgraduate architecture programme for the 2026–27 academic session, reiterating the B.Arch/equivalent and 50% eligibility baseline.

Admission is not determined by an entrance score alone at every institution. Under the COA guideline, competent admission authorities may assign weight to the PG entrance test, qualifying-examination score and other processes such as personal interviews, and must display the merit list transparently. Applicants should therefore follow the current notice of each target university or counselling authority for application dates, accepted test scores, portfolio/interview requirements, seat matrix and fees.
For 2026–27, dates and processes are time-sensitive. Use the current COA PGETA materials and the chosen institution’s official admissions notice rather than relying on a previous-year calendar.
bullets: Meet the B.Arch/equivalent and marks requirement.
Qualify the prescribed PG architecture entrance test.
Apply separately through the relevant university, institute or counselling authority where required.
Check how that authority weights entrance performance, academic record and any interview or other selection component.

sections.heading: What can follow an M.Arch
paragraphs: An M.Arch can support deeper specialization in design, building science, sustainability, urbanism, conservation, digital-building technologies, construction management, research and related architectural fields, depending on the programme chosen. COA’s framework explicitly frames postgraduate education around advanced specialization and supports research, development, conservation and innovation activities.

A research pathway is also available: under the UGC Ph.D. Regulations, 2022, a candidate who has completed a qualifying master’s degree may seek Ph.D. admission with at least 55% aggregate marks or equivalent grade, subject to the regulations and the admitting higher-education institution’s own selection process. This is a general Ph.D. eligibility rule, not an automatic M.Arch-to-Ph.D. admission guarantee.
bullets: Specialized professional practice or design/research roles aligned with the M.Arch concentration.
Doctoral research (Ph.D.), subject to UGC eligibility and institution-specific entrance/selection rules.
Academic and research-oriented progression where the relevant institution’s recruitment requirements are met.

useful_links.label: COA: Minimum Standards of Architectural Education Guidelines for Post-Graduate Degree Programme, 2022
